(P6375) == * {'text': '高知県香美市香北町韮生野243-ｲ', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} [[JA:大川上美良布神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11435126}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine | 画像=[[File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) | 所在地={{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kami|Q877689}} Kahoku-chō Nirōno 243-i | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神= {{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=(Tradition) Reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} | 例祭={{jalink|July 27|Q2719}}–{{jalink|July 28|Q2718|28}} (Summer festival)
{{jalink|November 2|Q2996}}–{{jalink|November 3|Q2995|3}} (Autumn festival) | 本殿=Three-bay {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Ōkawakami
Birafu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine''' (おおかわかみびらふじんじゃ)In 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' the name is given simply as "Birafu Shrine," with "Ōkawakami" described as an honorific title. The torii plaque and Kōchi Prefecture cultural property registration use "Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine," the haiden plaque reads "Shō-ichii Birafu Shrine," and the information board reads "Shō-ichii Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine." is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kahoku-chō|Q11667146}}, {{jalink|Kami|Q877689}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed shrine}}, and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. The shrine buildings are adorned with exquisite carvings by master craftsmen, and at the time of their construction the shrine was called "{{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330|Tosa}} {{jalink|Nikkō Tōshōgū|Q696641|Nikkō}}." ===Enshrined deities=== *Principal deity: '''{{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}}''' *Combined enshrinement: {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Ōmono-nushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Tamakushihime|Q11572681|Ikutamayori-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Suetsumimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushimikata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Iikatasu-mi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Himuka-Kamudo-Birafu-hime|Q97478962|Birafu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ===Overview=== The shrine is located approximately 100 meters north of the traffic signal in front of {{jalink|Michi-no-Eki Birafu|Q11641461}} along {{jalink|National Route 195|Q6156911}}. "Birafu" (びらふ), which gives the shrine its name, can also be read as "mira," an archaic word for {{jalink|garlic chives|Q42285}}. The local place name is Nirōno (にろうの), and "nirō" can also be read as "nirafu," sharing the same meaning as "birafu." The shrine's founding is said to date to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}}, though this remains in the realm of tradition. According to old records, there are descriptions indicating the shrine existed before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 8 ({{jalink|841|Q24870}}) in the early {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), also lists the shrine as one of four minor shrines in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Kami District|Q11667715}}. In antiquity, the shrine was also called "Kawakami Daimyōjin" or "Ōkawakamigū," and served as the sōchinju (head tutelary shrine) of the 50 villages of Nirō-gō. At the end of the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} period, on {{jalink|December 2 (old calendar)|Q840458|the 2nd day of the 12th month}} of {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}} 5 ({{jalink|January 11|Q2247}}, {{jalink|1853|Q7666}}), the shrine received by imperial decree the highest {{jalink|shrine rank|Q11591025}} of Shō-ichii (Senior First Rank). In the precinct stands a giant {{jalink|cryptomeria|Q147388|cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 4 ({{jalink|1747|Q7596}}) during the mid-{{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|kyōgen|Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{jalink|Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055}} :Two {{jalink|dōtaku|Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{jalink|centimeter|Q174728|cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{jalink|Monobe River|Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{jalink|September 1|Q2859}}, {{jalink|1934|Q18714}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{jalink|Keiō|Q776775}} 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}) and completed in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 2 ({{jalink|1869|Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{jalink|kokerabuki|kokera-buki}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{jalink|1971|Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{jalink|Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{jalink|Japan|Q17}}ese and {{jalink|China|Q29520|Chinese}} {{jalink|historical tales|Q1210892}}. Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{jalink|1953|Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}}, {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{jalink|March 28|Q2458}}, {{jalink|2003|Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, {{jalink|1970|Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{jalink|May 2|Q2545}}, {{jalink|2017|Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:need translation]]

(P6375) == * {'text': '高知県香美市香北町韮生野243-ｲ', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} [[JA:大川上美良布神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11435126}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine | 画像=[[File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) | 所在地={{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kami|Q877689}} Kahoku-chō Nirōno 243-i | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神= {{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=(Tradition) Reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} | 例祭={{jalink|July 27|Q2719}}–{{jalink|July 28|Q2718|28}} (Summer festival)
{{jalink|November 2|Q2996}}–{{jalink|November 3|Q2995|3}} (Autumn festival) | 本殿=Three-bay {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Ōkawakami
Birafu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine''' (おおかわかみびらふじんじゃ)In 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' the name is given simply as "Birafu Shrine," with "Ōkawakami" described as an honorific title. The torii plaque and Kōchi Prefecture cultural property registration use "Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine," the haiden plaque reads "Shō-ichii Birafu Shrine," and the information board reads "Shō-ichii Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine." is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kahoku-chō|Q11667146}}, {{jalink|Kami|Q877689}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed shrine}}, and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. The shrine buildings are adorned with exquisite carvings by master craftsmen, and at the time of their construction the shrine was called "{{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330|Tosa}} {{jalink|Nikkō Tōshōgū|Q696641|Nikkō}}." ===Enshrined deities=== *Principal deity: '''{{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}}''' *Combined enshrinement: {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Ōmono-nushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Tamakushihime|Q11572681|Ikutamayori-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Suetsumimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushimikata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Iikatasu-mi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Himuka-Kamudo-Birafu-hime|Q97478962|Birafu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ===Overview=== The shrine is located approximately 100 meters north of the traffic signal in front of {{jalink|Michi-no-Eki Birafu|Q11641461}} along {{jalink|National Route 195|Q6156911}}. "Birafu" (びらふ), which gives the shrine its name, can also be read as "mira," an archaic word for {{jalink|garlic chives|Q42285}}. The local place name is Nirōno (にろうの), and "nirō" can also be read as "nirafu," sharing the same meaning as "birafu." The shrine's founding is said to date to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}}, though this remains in the realm of tradition. According to old records, there are descriptions indicating the shrine existed before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 8 ({{jalink|841|Q24870}}) in the early {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), also lists the shrine as one of four minor shrines in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Kami District|Q11667715}}. In antiquity, the shrine was also called "Kawakami Daimyōjin" or "Ōkawakamigū," and served as the sōchinju (head tutelary shrine) of the 50 villages of Nirō-gō. At the end of the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} period, on {{jalink|December 2 (old calendar)|Q840458|the 2nd day of the 12th month}} of {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}} 5 ({{jalink|January 11|Q2247}}, {{jalink|1853|Q7666}}), the shrine received by imperial decree the highest {{jalink|shrine rank|Q11591025}} of Shō-ichii (Senior First Rank). In the precinct stands a giant {{jalink|cryptomeria|Q147388|cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 4 ({{jalink|1747|Q7596}}) during the mid-{{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|kyōgen|Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{jalink|Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055}} :Two {{jalink|dōtaku|Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{jalink|centimeter|Q174728|cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{jalink|Monobe River|Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{jalink|September 1|Q2859}}, {{jalink|1934|Q18714}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{jalink|Keiō|Q776775}} 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}) and completed in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 2 ({{jalink|1869|Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{jalink|kokerabuki|kokera-buki}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{jalink|1971|Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{jalink|Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{jalink|Japan|Q17}}ese and {{jalink|China|Q29520|Chinese}} {{jalink|historical tales|Q1210892}}. Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{jalink|1953|Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}}, {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{jalink|March 28|Q2458}}, {{jalink|2003|Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, {{jalink|1970|Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{jalink|May 2|Q2545}}, {{jalink|2017|Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:need translation]]

Ollama and DeepSeek were too bad to actually use. see categorization since some of them are very questionable, especially if there was a massive decrease in page size or the {{jawiki| template is minimal or absent on the page

The shrine's founding is said to date to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}}, though this remains in the realm of tradition. According to old records, there are descriptions indicating the shrine existed before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 8 ({{jalink|841|Q24870}}) in the early {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), also lists the shrine as one of four minor shrines in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Kami District|Q11667715}}. In antiquity, the shrine was also called "Kawakami Daimyōjin" or "Ōkawakamigū," and served as the sōchinju (head tutelary shrine) of the 50 villages of Nirō-gō. At the end of the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} period, on {{jalink|December 2 (old calendar)|Q840458|the 2nd day of the 12th month}} of {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}} 5 ({{jalink|January 11|Q2247}}, {{jalink|1853|Q7666}}), the shrine received by imperial decree the highest {{jalink|shrine rank|Q11591025}} of Shō-ichii (Senior First Rank). In the precinct stands a giant {{jalink|cryptomeria|Q147388|cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 4 ({{jalink|1747|Q7596}}) during the mid-{{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|kyōgen|Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{jalink|Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055}} :Two {{jalink|dōtaku|Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{jalink|centimeter|Q174728|cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{jalink|Monobe River|Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{jalink|September 1|Q2859}}, {{jalink|1934|Q18714}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{jalink|Keiō|Q776775}} 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}) and completed in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 2 ({{jalink|1869|Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{jalink|kokerabuki|kokera-buki}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{jalink|1971|Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{jalink|Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{jalink|Japan|Q17}}ese and {{jalink|China|Q29520|Chinese}} {{jalink|historical tales|Q1210892}}. Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{jalink|1953|Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}}, {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{jalink|March 28|Q2458}}, {{jalink|2003|Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, {{jalink|1970|Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{jalink|May 2|Q2545}}, {{jalink|2017|Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:need translation]]

Birafu Shrine | 画像=[[File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) | 所在地={{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kami|Q877689}} Kahoku-chō Nirōno 243-i | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神= {{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=(Tradition) Reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} | 例祭={{jalink|July 27|Q2719}}–{{jalink|July 28|Q2718|28}} (Summer festival)
{{jalink|November 2|Q2996}}–{{jalink|November 3|Q2995|3}} (Autumn festival) | 本殿=Three-bay {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Ōkawakami
Birafu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine''' (おおかわかみびらふじんじゃ)In 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' the name is given simply as "Birafu Shrine," with "Ōkawakami" described as an honorific title. The torii plaque and Kōchi Prefecture cultural property registration use "Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine," the haiden plaque reads "Shō-ichii Birafu Shrine," and the information board reads "Shō-ichii Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine." is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kahoku-chō|Q11667146}}, {{jalink|Kami|Q877689}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed shrine}}, and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. The shrine buildings are adorned with exquisite carvings by master craftsmen, and at the time of their construction the shrine was called "{{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330|Tosa}} {{jalink|Nikkō Tōshōgū|Q696641|Nikkō}}." ===Enshrined deities=== *Principal deity: '''{{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}}''' *Combined enshrinement: {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Ōmono-nushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Tamakushihime|Q11572681|Ikutamayori-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Suetsumimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushimikata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Iikatasu-mi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Himuka-Kamudo-Birafu-hime|Q97478962|Birafu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ===Overview=== The shrine is located approximately 100 meters north of the traffic signal in front of {{jalink|Michi-no-Eki Birafu|Q11641461}} along {{jalink|National Route 195|Q6156911}}. "Birafu" (びらふ), which gives the shrine its name, can also be read as "mira," an archaic word for {{jalink|garlic chives|Q42285}}. The local place name is Nirōno (にろうの), and "nirō" can also be read as "nirafu," sharing the same meaning as "birafu." The shrine's founding is said to date to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}}, though this remains in the realm of tradition. According to old records, there are descriptions indicating the shrine existed before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 8 ({{jalink|841|Q24870}}) in the early {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), also lists the shrine as one of four minor shrines in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Kami District|Q11667715}}. In antiquity, the shrine was also called "Kawakami Daimyōjin" or "Ōkawakamigū," and served as the sōchinju (head tutelary shrine) of the 50 villages of Nirō-gō. At the end of the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} period, on {{jalink|December 2 (old calendar)|Q840458|the 2nd day of the 12th month}} of {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}} 5 ({{jalink|January 11|Q2247}}, {{jalink|1853|Q7666}}), the shrine received by imperial decree the highest {{jalink|shrine rank|Q11591025}} of Shō-ichii (Senior First Rank). In the precinct stands a giant {{jalink|cryptomeria|Q147388|cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 4 ({{jalink|1747|Q7596}}) during the mid-{{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|kyōgen|Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{jalink|Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055}} :Two {{jalink|dōtaku|Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{jalink|centimeter|Q174728|cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{jalink|Monobe River|Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{jalink|September 1|Q2859}}, {{jalink|1934|Q18714}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{jalink|Keiō|Q776775}} 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}) and completed in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 2 ({{jalink|1869|Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{jalink|kokerabuki|kokera-buki}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{jalink|1971|Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{jalink|Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{jalink|Japan|Q17}}ese and {{jalink|China|Q29520|Chinese}} {{jalink|historical tales|Q1210892}}. Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{jalink|1953|Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}}, {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{jalink|March 28|Q2458}}, {{jalink|2003|Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, {{jalink|1970|Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{jalink|May 2|Q2545}}, {{jalink|2017|Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:need translation]]

Birafu Shrine | 画像=[[File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) | 所在地={{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kami|Q877689}} Kahoku-chō Nirōno 243-i | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神= {{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=(Tradition) Reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} | 例祭={{jalink|July 27|Q2719}}–{{jalink|July 28|Q2718|28}} (Summer festival)
{{jalink|November 2|Q2996}}–{{jalink|November 3|Q2995|3}} (Autumn festival) | 本殿=Three-bay {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Ōkawakami
Birafu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine''' (おおかわかみびらふじんじゃ)In 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' the name is given simply as "Birafu Shrine," with "Ōkawakami" described as an honorific title. The torii plaque and Kōchi Prefecture cultural property registration use "Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine," the haiden plaque reads "Shō-ichii Birafu Shrine," and the information board reads "Shō-ichii Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine." is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kahoku-chō|Q11667146}}, {{jalink|Kami|Q877689}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed shrine}}, and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. The shrine buildings are adorned with exquisite carvings by master craftsmen, and at the time of their construction the shrine was called "{{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330|Tosa}} {{jalink|Nikkō Tōshōgū|Q696641|Nikkō}}." ===Enshrined deities=== *Principal deity: '''{{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}}''' *Combined enshrinement: {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Ōmono-nushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Tamakushihime|Q11572681|Ikutamayori-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Suetsumimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushimikata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Iikatasu-mi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Himuka-Kamudo-Birafu-hime|Q97478962|Birafu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ===Overview=== The shrine is located approximately 100 meters north of the traffic signal in front of {{jalink|Michi-no-Eki Birafu|Q11641461}} along {{jalink|National Route 195|Q6156911}}. "Birafu" (びらふ), which gives the shrine its name, can also be read as "mira," an archaic word for {{jalink|garlic chives|Q42285}}. The local place name is Nirōno (にろうの), and "nirō" can also be read as "nirafu," sharing the same meaning as "birafu." The shrine's founding is said to date to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}}, though this remains in the realm of tradition. According to old records, there are descriptions indicating the shrine existed before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 8 ({{jalink|841|Q24870}}) in the early {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), also lists the shrine as one of four minor shrines in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Kami District|Q11667715}}. In antiquity, the shrine was also called "Kawakami Daimyōjin" or "Ōkawakamigū," and served as the sōchinju (head tutelary shrine) of the 50 villages of Nirō-gō. At the end of the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} period, on {{jalink|December 2 (old calendar)|Q840458|the 2nd day of the 12th month}} of {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}} 5 ({{jalink|January 11|Q2247}}, {{jalink|1853|Q7666}}), the shrine received by imperial decree the highest {{jalink|shrine rank|Q11591025}} of Shō-ichii (Senior First Rank). In the precinct stands a giant {{jalink|cryptomeria|Q147388|cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 4 ({{jalink|1747|Q7596}}) during the mid-{{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|kyōgen|Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{jalink|Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055}} :Two {{jalink|dōtaku|Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{jalink|centimeter|Q174728|cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{jalink|Monobe River|Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{jalink|September 1|Q2859}}, {{jalink|1934|Q18714}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{jalink|Keiō|Q776775}} 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}) and completed in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 2 ({{jalink|1869|Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{jalink|kokerabuki|kokera-buki}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{jalink|1971|Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{jalink|Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{jalink|Japan|Q17}}ese and {{jalink|China|Q29520|Chinese}} {{jalink|historical tales|Q1210892}}. Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{jalink|1953|Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}}, {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{jalink|March 28|Q2458}}, {{jalink|2003|Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, {{jalink|1970|Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{jalink|May 2|Q2545}}, {{jalink|2017|Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]]

The torii plaque and Kōchi Prefecture cultural property registration use "Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine," the haiden plaque reads "Shō-ichii Birafu Shrine," and the information board reads "Shō-ichii Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine." is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kahoku-chō|Q11667146}}, {{jalink|Kami|Q877689}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed shrine}}, and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. The shrine buildings are adorned with exquisite carvings by master craftsmen, and at the time of their construction the shrine was called "{{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330|Tosa}} {{jalink|Nikkō Tōshōgū|Q696641|Nikkō}}." ===Enshrined deities=== *Principal deity: '''{{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}}''' *Combined enshrinement: {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Ōmono-nushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Tamakushihime|Q11572681|Ikutamayori-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Suetsumimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushimikata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Iikatasu-mi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Himuka-Kamudo-Birafu-hime|Q97478962|Birafu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ===Overview=== The shrine is located approximately 100 meters north of the traffic signal in front of {{jalink|Michi-no-Eki Birafu|Q11641461}} along {{jalink|National Route 195|Q6156911}}. "Birafu" (びらふ), which gives the shrine its name, can also be read as "mira," an archaic word for {{jalink|garlic chives|Q42285}}. The local place name is Nirōno (にろうの), and "nirō" can also be read as "nirafu," sharing the same meaning as "birafu." The shrine's founding is said to date to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}}, though this remains in the realm of tradition. According to old records, there are descriptions indicating the shrine existed before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 8 ({{jalink|841|Q24870}}) in the early {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), also lists the shrine as one of four minor shrines in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Kami District|Q11667715}}. In antiquity, the shrine was also called "Kawakami Daimyōjin" or "Ōkawakamigū," and served as the sōchinju (head tutelary shrine) of the 50 villages of Nirō-gō. At the end of the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} period, on {{jalink|December 2 (old calendar)|Q840458|the 2nd day of the 12th month}} of {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}} 5 ({{jalink|January 11|Q2247}}, {{jalink|1853|Q7666}}), the shrine received by imperial decree the highest {{jalink|shrine rank|Q11591025}} of Shō-ichii (Senior First Rank). In the precinct stands a giant {{jalink|cryptomeria|Q147388|cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 4 ({{jalink|1747|Q7596}}) during the mid-{{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|kyōgen|Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{jalink|Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055}} :Two {{jalink|dōtaku|Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{jalink|centimeter|Q174728|cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{jalink|Monobe River|Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{jalink|September 1|Q2859}}, {{jalink|1934|Q18714}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{jalink|Keiō|Q776775}} 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}) and completed in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 2 ({{jalink|1869|Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{jalink|kokerabuki|kokera-buki}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{jalink|1971|Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{jalink|Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{jalink|Japan|Q17}}ese and {{jalink|China|Q29520|Chinese}} {{jalink|historical tales|Q1210892}}. Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{jalink|1953|Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}}, {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{jalink|March 28|Q2458}}, {{jalink|2003|Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, {{jalink|1970|Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{jalink|May 2|Q2545}}, {{jalink|2017|Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]][[Category:qq]]

Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{jalink|Japan|Q17}}ese and {{jalink|China|Q29520|Chinese}} {{jalink|historical tales|Q1210892}}. Designated {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}}, {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{jalink|March 28|Q2458}}, {{jalink|2003|Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, {{jalink|1970|Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{jalink|May 2|Q2545}}, {{jalink|2017|Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

(P6375) == * {'text': '高知県香美市香北町韮生野243-ｲ', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} [[JA:大川上美良布神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11435126}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine | 画像=[[File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) | 所在地={{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kami|Q877689}} Kahoku-chō Nirōno 243-i | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神= {{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=(Tradition) Reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Former {{jalink|kensha}} | 例祭={{jalink|July 27|Q2719}}–{{jalink|July 28|Q2718|28}} (Summer festival)
{{jalink|November 2|Q2996}}–{{jalink|November 3|Q2995|3}} (Autumn festival) | 本殿=Three-bay {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Ōkawakami
Birafu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine''' (おおかわかみびらふじんじゃ)In 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' the name is given simply as "Birafu Shrine," with "Ōkawakami" described as an honorific title. The torii plaque and Kōchi Prefecture cultural property registration use "Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine," the haiden plaque reads "Shō-ichii Birafu Shrine," and the information board reads "Shō-ichii Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine." is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kahoku-chō|Q11667146}}, {{jalink|Kami|Q877689}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is an {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki-listed shrine}}, and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|kensha}}. The shrine buildings are adorned with exquisite carvings by master craftsmen, and at the time of their construction the shrine was called "{{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330|Tosa}} {{jalink|Nikkō Tōshōgū|Q696641|Nikkō}}." ===Enshrined deities=== *Principal deity: '''{{jalink|Ōtataneko|Q55536798|Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}}''' *Combined enshrinement: {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Ōmono-nushi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Tamakushihime|Q11572681|Ikutamayori-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Suetsumimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushimikata-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Iikatasu-mi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Himuka-Kamudo-Birafu-hime|Q97478962|Birafu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} ===Overview=== The shrine is located approximately 100 meters north of the traffic signal in front of {{jalink|Michi-no-Eki Birafu|Q11641461}} along {{jalink|National Route 195|Q6156911}}. "Birafu" (びらふ), which gives the shrine its name, can also be read as "mira," an archaic word for {{jalink|garlic chives|Q42285}}. The local place name is Nirōno (にろうの), and "nirō" can also be read as "nirafu," sharing the same meaning as "birafu." The shrine's founding is said to date to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}}, though this remains in the realm of tradition. According to old records, there are descriptions indicating the shrine existed before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 8 ({{jalink|841|Q24870}}) in the early {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}},' compiled in {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), also lists the shrine as one of four minor shrines in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Kami District|Q11667715}}. In antiquity, the shrine was also called "Kawakami Daimyōjin" or "Ōkawakamigū," and served as the sōchinju (head tutelary shrine) of the 50 villages of Nirō-gō. At the end of the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} period, on {{jalink|December 2 (old calendar)|Q840458|the 2nd day of the 12th month}} of {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}} 5 ({{jalink|January 11|Q2247}}, {{jalink|1853|Q7666}}), the shrine received by imperial decree the highest {{jalink|shrine rank|Q11591025}} of Shō-ichii (Senior First Rank). In the precinct stands a giant {{jalink|cryptomeria|Q147388|cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 4 ({{jalink|1747|Q7596}}) during the mid-{{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|kyōgen|Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{jalink|Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055}} :Two {{jalink|dōtaku|Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{jalink|centimeter|Q174728|cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{jalink|Monobe River|Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{jalink|September 1|Q2859}}, {{jalink|1934|Q18714}} ({{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{jalink|Keiō|Q776775}} 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}) and completed in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 2 ({{jalink|1869|Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{jalink|kokerabuki|kokera-buki}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{jalink|1971|Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{jalink|Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{jalink|Japan|Q17}}ese and {{jalink|China|Q29520|Chinese}} {{jalink|historical tales|Q1210892}}. Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{jalink|1953|Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}}, {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{jalink|March 28|Q2458}}, {{jalink|2003|Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{jalink|January 16|Q2261}}, {{jalink|1970|Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{jalink|May 2|Q2545}}, {{jalink|2017|Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

(P6375) == * {'text': '高知県香美市香北町韮生野243-ｲ', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} [[JA:大川上美良布神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11435126}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine | 画像=[[File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|280px]]
Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) | 所在地={{ill|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093|WD=Q134093}}{{ill|lt=Kami|Q877689|WD=Q877689}} Kahoku-chō Nirōno 243-i | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神= {{ill|lt=Ōtataneko|Q55536798|WD=Q55536798|lt=Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=(Tradition) Reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} | 社格={{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
Former {{ill||lt=kensha|UNKNOWN}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 27|Q2719|WD=Q2719}}–{{ill|lt=July 28|Q2718|WD=Q2718|lt=28}} (Summer festival)
{{ill|lt=November 2|Q2996|WD=Q2996}}–{{ill|lt=November 3|Q2995|WD=Q2995|lt=3}} (Autumn festival) | 本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Ōkawakami
Birafu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine''' (おおかわかみびらふじんじゃ)In 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' the name is given simply as "Birafu Shrine," with "Ōkawakami" described as an honorific title. The torii plaque and Kōchi Prefecture cultural property registration use "Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine," the haiden plaque reads "Shō-ichii Birafu Shrine," and the information board reads "Shō-ichii Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine." is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Kahoku-chō|Q11667146|WD=Q11667146}}, {{ill|lt=Kami|Q877689|WD=Q877689}}, {{ill|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093|WD=Q134093}}. It is an {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank}} was {{ill||lt=kensha|UNKNOWN}}. The shrine buildings are adorned with exquisite carvings by master craftsmen, and at the time of their construction the shrine was called "{{ill|lt=Tosa Province|Q857330|WD=Q857330|lt=Tosa}} {{ill|lt=Nikkō Tōshōgū|Q696641|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō}}." ===Enshrined deities=== *Principal deity: '''{{ill|lt=Ōtataneko|Q55536798|WD=Q55536798|lt=Ōtata-neko-no-Mikoto}}''' *Combined enshrinement: {{ill||lt=Ōmononushi|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Tamakushihime|Q11572681|WD=Q11572681|lt=Ikutamayori-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill||lt=Suetsumimi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Kushimikata-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Iikatasu-mi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Himuka-Kamudo-Birafu-hime|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962|lt=Birafu-hime-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill||lt=Takemikazuchi|UNKNOWN}} ===Overview=== The shrine is located approximately 100 meters north of the traffic signal in front of {{ill|lt=Michi-no-Eki Birafu|Q11641461|WD=Q11641461}} along {{ill|lt=National Route 195|Q6156911|WD=Q6156911}}. "Birafu" (びらふ), which gives the shrine its name, can also be read as "mira," an archaic word for {{ill|lt=garlic chives|Q42285|WD=Q42285}}. The local place name is Nirōno (にろうの), and "nirō" can also be read as "nirafu," sharing the same meaning as "birafu." The shrine's founding is said to date to the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}}, though this remains in the realm of tradition. According to old records, there are descriptions indicating the shrine existed before {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 8 ({{ill|lt=841|Q24870|WD=Q24870}}) in the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}. The '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}},' compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), also lists the shrine as one of four minor shrines in {{ill|lt=Tosa Province|Q857330|WD=Q857330}} {{ill|lt=Kami District|Q11667715|WD=Q11667715}}. In antiquity, the shrine was also called "Kawakami Daimyōjin" or "Ōkawakamigū," and served as the sōchinju (head tutelary shrine) of the 50 villages of Nirō-gō. At the end of the {{ill|lt=Bakumatsu|Q804558|WD=Q804558}} period, on {{ill|lt=December 2 (old calendar)|Q840458|WD=Q840458|lt=the 2nd day of the 12th month}} of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125}} 5 ({{ill|lt=January 11|Q2247|WD=Q2247}}, {{ill|lt=1853|Q7666|WD=Q7666}}), the shrine received by imperial decree the highest {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} of Shō-ichii (Senior First Rank). In the precinct stands a giant {{ill|lt=cryptomeria|Q147388|WD=Q147388|lt=cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1747|Q7596|WD=Q7596}}) during the mid-{{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, {{ill|lt=kyōgen|Q944671|WD=Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{ill|lt=Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055|WD=Q48752055}} :Two {{ill|lt=dōtaku|Q2312868|WD=Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{ill|lt=centimeter|Q174728|WD=Q174728|lt=cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{ill|lt=Monobe River|Q11570692|WD=Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{ill|lt=September 1|Q2859|WD=Q2859}}, {{ill|lt=1934|Q18714|WD=Q18714}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|WD=Q776775}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1865|Q7708|WD=Q7708}}) and completed in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1869|Q7720|WD=Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{ill||lt=kokerabuki|UNKNOWN}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{ill|lt=1971|Q2475|WD=Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{ill|lt=Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093|WD=Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17}}ese and {{ill|lt=China|Q29520|WD=Q29520|lt=Chinese}} {{ill|lt=historical tales|Q1210892|WD=Q1210892}}. Designated {{ill|lt=January 16|Q2261|WD=Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{ill|lt=1953|Q18585|WD=Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{ill|lt=March 29|Q2459|WD=Q2459}}, {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{ill|lt=March 28|Q2458|WD=Q2458}}, {{ill|lt=2003|Q1986|WD=Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{ill|lt=January 16|Q2261|WD=Q2261}}, {{ill|lt=1970|Q2474|WD=Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545}}, {{ill|lt=2017|Q25290|WD=Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

In the precinct stands a giant {{ill|lt=cryptomeria|Q147388|WD=Q147388|lt=cryptomeria}} tree said to be 1,000 years old, designated as the sacred tree. Its height is approximately 40 meters. === Precinct === File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, honden.jpg|Honden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 07.jpg|Honden close-up File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|Haiden (Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu-jinja, tsuyaden-1.jpg|Tsūyaden (Kami City designated cultural property) File:Okawakami Birafu jinja 10.jpg|Sacred cryptomeria tree ===Autumn Grand Festival=== Held annually over two days from November 2 to 3. A sacred procession called "onabare," designated as a Kōchi Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property, takes place. During the onabare, events include bō-uchi (stick fighting), goban-furi (go-board shaking), a portable-shrine procession, noses-on-stilts characters (hanadaka), a children's procession (chigo gyōretsu), and torimō/haguma (feathered poles approximately 5 meters tall adorned with bird feathers and fur). Within the precinct, there is a tsūyaden (night-vigil hall) containing an indoor revolving stage, and since {{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1747|Q7596|WD=Q7596}}) during the mid-{{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, {{ill|lt=kyōgen|Q944671|WD=Q944671}} performances have been staged there annually. === Cultural properties === === Important Art Objects (nationally recognized) === *{{ill|lt=Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells / unearthed at Nirōno, Birafu Village, Kami District, Kōchi Prefecture|Q48752055|WD=Q48752055}} :Two {{ill|lt=dōtaku|Q2312868|WD=Q2312868}} (bronze bells) measuring 75.7 {{ill|lt=centimeter|Q174728|WD=Q174728|lt=cm}} and 67 cm in height. They were unearthed in the former Ioroi Village in the western part of Kahoku-chō and are said to have been dedicated to this shrine as sacred treasures. Known as the "Nirō no Furigane" (Falling Bells of Nirō), they were reportedly used in rain-invocation rituals at the {{ill|lt=Monobe River|Q11570692|WD=Q11570692}}. Recognized on {{ill|lt=September 1|Q2859|WD=Q2859}}, {{ill|lt=1934|Q18714|WD=Q18714}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 9){{Cite wikisource|和書|title=Recognition of Objects Related to the Preservation of Important Art Objects|wslink= 重要美術品等ノ保存ニ関スル物件認定_(昭和9年文部省告示第245号)|author=Ministry of Education Notification No. 245 of 1934|date=September 1, 1934|wslanguage=ja}}. === Kōchi Prefecture designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties **Shrine buildings **: The haiden, heiden, and honden were begun in {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|WD=Q776775}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1865|Q7708|WD=Q7708}}) and completed in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1869|Q7720|WD=Q7720}}). They feature chidori-hafu (cusped gables) and kara-hafu (undulating gables) for visual variation. Originally roofed with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kokerabuki|UNKNOWN}} (thin wooden shingles), the roofs were replaced with copper sheeting in {{ill|lt=1971|Q2475|WD=Q2475}} (Shōwa 46). Under master carpenter Shimamura Yasutaka, four carpenters competed against one another in their work. On the front center of the honden, Shimamura Yasutaka carved {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}; Hara Uhei carved {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}}; on the right, Sakaide Sadanosuke carved {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}}; and on the left, Betsuyaku Mokusaburō carved envoys from the {{ill|lt=Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q1376093|WD=Q1376093}}. Other surfaces are also carved with scenes from {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17}}ese and {{ill|lt=China|Q29520|WD=Q29520|lt=Chinese}} {{ill|lt=historical tales|Q1210892|WD=Q1210892}}. Designated {{ill|lt=January 16|Q2261|WD=Q2261}}, Shōwa 28 ({{ill|lt=1953|Q18585|WD=Q18585}}). ** Kesadasuki-mon bronze bells, 2 pieces **: Same as the Important Art Objects above. Designated {{ill|lt=March 29|Q2459|WD=Q2459}}, {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 14). * Intangible folk cultural properties ** Sacred procession of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine **: Designated {{ill|lt=March 28|Q2458|WD=Q2458}}, {{ill|lt=2003|Q1986|WD=Q1986}} (Heisei 15). === Kami City designated cultural properties === * Tangible cultural properties ** Revolving stage of Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine tsūyaden **: Designated {{ill|lt=January 16|Q2261|WD=Q2261}}, {{ill|lt=1970|Q2474|WD=Q2474}} (Shōwa 45) (former Kahoku-chō). === Registered Tangible Cultural Property (nationally registered) === * Shinko (sacred storehouse) *: Registered {{ill|lt=May 2|Q2545|WD=Q2545}}, {{ill|lt=2017|Q25290|WD=Q25290}} (Heisei 29)[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/222404 Cultural Heritage Online]. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===References=== *Kōchi Prefecture High School Education Research Association, History Division, ed., 'Kōchi-ken no Rekishi Sanpo,' Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2006, pp. 146-148 *On-site information board ===External links=== {{Commonscat|Okawakami Birafu-jinja}} *[https://www.city.kami.lg.jp/map/kawakamisama.html Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine] - Kami City Website {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおかわかみひらふしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōkawakami Birafu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tosa Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:香美市の歴史]] [[Category:高知県の旧県社]] [[Category:土佐国の式内社]] [[Category:高知県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:香美市の建築物]] [[Category:高知県の登録有形文化財]] [[Category:高知県内の市町村指定有形文化財]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]
